children.text: The management of the Panama Canal watershed is articulated through a technical language of forecasts, basin modeling, and infrastructure planning. In official discourse, water is treated as a quantifiable resource that can be redistributed across time through reservoirs, dams, and hydrological projections.
This epistemology assumes that territory can be rendered legible through measurement: rainfall becomes data, rivers become supply, and rural basins become operational extensions of the Canal system.

However, this mode of knowledge does not exist alone. It is continuously confronted by other forms of knowing territory—forms that do not separate water from land, or land from memory, or memory from the conditions of survival.

In the documentary materials and interviews assembled in this project, Digna appears within this tension. Her account does not translate easily into the language of planning or policy. It registers instead as lived anticipation: a relationship to land shaped by the possibility of displacement that precedes its formal announcement.

What emerges is not a single narrative of the watershed, but a field of competing epistemologies. One organizes water through future capacity and infrastructure design. The other experiences water through uncertainty, continuity, and the fragility of dwelling within a territory that is being reclassified in advance of intervention.
The watershed, in this sense, is not only a hydrological unit. It is a site where different ways of knowing territory are placed into confrontation without resolution.
